title: Alnylam — PH — Synthesis
html:
Alnylam PH1 Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-25 Studies analyzed: OXLUMO PET Baseline (Q4 2023), OXLUMO PET Final Report (Q3 2025), OXLUMO ATU Wave 1 Full Report (Dec 2024), OXLUMO PET Interim (Aug 2025) Data sources: ZoomRx SharePoint deliverables + Alnylam/Novo Nordisk earnings and regulatory announcements

1. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: OXLUMO's First-Mover Advantage Is Real but Eroding — Rivfloza Reps Are Catching Up

PET Baseline Q4 2023: OXLUMO was the only promoted PH1 therapy in the market. Reach was 81% overall (84% target, 76% off-target). No Rivfloza comparison possible as Rivfloza was approved only in September 2023 and promotional activity was not yet measured. OXLUMO reps rated highly above rare disease industry standards across all attributes. ~60% of HCPs reported improved perception and likelihood to Rx post-interaction. Overall sales call quality: 79%.

PET Q3 2025 (Final Report): Head-to-head competitive tracking introduced (29 OXLUMO interactions vs 20 Rivfloza interactions). OXLUMO reach dropped to 47% (from 81% in baseline) — a direct consequence of Rivfloza splitting share of voice. However, OXLUMO maintained: higher overall sales call quality (83% vs Rivfloza), higher messaging effectiveness (ME 65% vs lower for Rivfloza), and stronger likelihood to recommend rep to colleague. BUT Rivfloza reps led in change in product perception — driven by novelty and new information. Rivfloza reps also closed more often on differentiating the product as PH1 treatment of choice.

PET Interim (Aug 2025, n=15): Early signal that Rivfloza reps are directionally catching up: "RIVFLOZA reps are more likely to positively impact likelihood to prescribe and perceptions of their product compared to OXLUMO reps." Rivfloza reps closing more often on product differentiation as treatment of choice. Sample size is small (directional only) but the trend is consistent with the final report.

What this means: OXLUMO's promotional dominance is transitioning from monopoly to competition. The first-mover advantage manifests as higher familiarity, stronger rep-to-colleague recommendations, and broader rep attribute ratings — but Rivfloza is generating more "change" in perceptions precisely because it's new. This is a natural competitive dynamic that will stabilize, but Alnylam cannot rely on inertia. The rep effectiveness metrics that matter most going forward are NOT overall quality (which OXLUMO wins easily) but rather change in product perception and closing on specific treatment decisions — areas where Rivfloza is gaining.

Pattern 2: The Access/Affordability Perception Gap Is Consistent Across Every Study

PET Baseline Q4 2023: "Lack of cost-effective treatment" was the #1 cited unmet need in PH1 diagnosis and treatment. Access/insurance/cost was among the top 3 aided topics discussed during OXLUMO sales details.

ATU Wave 1 (Dec 2024): High OOP costs are a greater barrier for OXLUMO than Rivfloza among BOTH nephrology and urology. Critical finding: "Physicians have better perceptions of Rivfloza's affordability and access even if they do not personally have experience with patients on Rivfloza." Lower OOP cost perceptions for OXLUMO are driven specifically by OXLUMO non-users. Among experienced OXLUMO users, OOP cost perceptions are better — experience corrects the gap, but non-users never get that correction. ATU recommendation: "Alnylam should prioritize highlighting their Patient Support Services."

PET Q3 2025: Rivfloza reps use formulary access/reimbursement sheets more often during high-impact interactions. HCPs cite insurance coverage and high OOP costs as top barriers for OXLUMO. The OX8 patient support message (94% pay nothing, 95% coverage) has moderate recall and effectiveness — it exists but isn't dominant enough to counter the Rivfloza access narrative.

What this means: The affordability/access perception problem is THE most consistent finding across 2 years and 4 studies. It's not a sampling artifact or a one-wave anomaly — it's structural. Novo Nordisk is proactively winning this narrative with access-focused promotional materials, while Alnylam's own access story (which is objectively strong) is underrepresented in promotional emphasis. The most concerning aspect: the perception gap is WORST among non-users and off-list HCPs, who are exactly the growth segment Alnylam needs to convert. ZoomRx should flag this as a longitudinal strategic insight in every Alnylam deliverable.

Pattern 3: The Low-Recall / High-Effectiveness Message Gap Is an Untapped Opportunity

PET Baseline Q4 2023: OXLUMO's efficacy messages (OX2: ILLUMINATE-A data, OX4: ILLUMINATE-B data) had recall of ~30% despite high motivation-to-Rx scores. The gap between recall and effectiveness was noted as an opportunity.

PET Q3 2025: The pattern persists and is now quantified precisely:

  • OX2 ("tested in severely affected, advanced kidney disease, dialysis patients"): 38% recall, 69% effectiveness
  • OX3 ("largest PH1 trial program"): 38% recall, 66% effectiveness
  • Both messages are more often recalled during HIGH-IMPACT interactions — they drive impact when heard, they're just not heard often enough

ATU Wave 1: The OXLUMO approval message ("first and only product approved for ALL AGES, ALL stages of kidney function") was the single most recalled message from rep interactions. But the severity data and trial program scope messages remain underleveraged.

What this means: There are two underleveraged OXLUMO messages (OX2 and OX3) that consistently show the same pattern — low recall but high effectiveness. This is the textbook PET insight: these messages WORK when delivered but aren't being delivered often enough. The practical recommendation is to increase rep training emphasis on these messages, particularly in interactions with off-list HCPs and urologists who may not know about OXLUMO's severity data or trial program breadth. For ZoomRx, this is a deliverable-quality insight that demonstrates PET ROI to Alnylam.

Pattern 4: Specialty Divergence — Nephrologists and Urologists Operate in Different Worlds

ATU Wave 1: Nephrologists are the primary caregivers and treatment decision makers for PH1. They are significantly more familiar with siRNA therapy options, more likely to order genetic testing, and more likely to prescribe OXLUMO. Urologists focus on stone management and are less familiar with PH1-specific treatments beyond supportive care or transplant. Key ATU finding: "Urologists are less familiar with treatment options beyond supportive care or transplantation."

Specific divergences:

  • Top clinical prescribing driver: Nephrologists = efficacy in lowering oxalate; Urologists = change in kidney stone events
  • Disease beliefs: Urologists consider stone formation a critical benchmark; less likely to agree PH1 can present at any age
  • Diagnosis: Nephrologists have higher genetic testing rates; Urologists focus on stone analysis and renal imaging
  • Treatment preference: 55% of nephrologists chose OXLUMO for pediatric patient; urologists more often chose supportive care only

PET Baseline Q4 2023: Academic HCPs (more often nephrologists) utilized journals/publications; community HCPs (more often urologists) relied on sales rep interactions. Community HCPs were MORE likely to increase OXLUMO Rx post-details (72% vs 45% for academic) — suggesting the sales force is particularly impactful for the less-informed segment.

PET Q3 2025: Community HCPs (53%) report more "high impact" OXLUMO interactions than academic HCPs (47%). Rep enthusiasm, PH1 knowledge, and competitor knowledge are higher predictors of high-impact interactions — all more impactful for community/urologist audiences.

What this means: There are effectively two distinct audiences for OXLUMO promotion: (1) Nephrologists who are familiar with PH1 and need competitive differentiation data vs Rivfloza, and (2) Urologists/community HCPs who need disease awareness education and fundamental PH1 treatment information. The PET and ATU should segment analysis by specialty more aggressively. The Alnylam commercial team may benefit from specialty-specific messaging strategies and potentially different call plans for nephrology vs urology. ZoomRx can propose this segmentation as a study design enhancement.

Pattern 5: OXLUMO's Quarterly Dosing Message Is the Anchor — But It's Not Sufficient Alone

PET Baseline Q4 2023: OX1 (indication/"first approved treatment for all ages with PH1") was the most recalled message (55%). Patient support (OX7) was second. The quarterly dosing element was embedded in the indication message.

PET Q3 2025: OX1 evolved to "only approved quarterly subcutaneous injection for PH1 (≥10kg) for patients of all ages and all kidney stages" — and remains the MOST recalled (66%) and MOST effective (ME 70%) OXLUMO message. It bundles: quarterly dosing + all ages + all kidney stages in a single message.

What this means: OX1 is OXLUMO's definitive anchor message and should remain the lead. It performs well because it bundles multiple competitive differentiators into one statement. However, the data shows that OX1 alone is not sufficient to drive high-impact interactions — high-impact interactions also require disease education (PH1 testing, genetic testing), severity data (OX2), and rep closing behaviors. The strategic insight: OX1 opens the door, but the depth messages (OX2, OX3, disease messages D4, D5) close the deal.

2. Thesis Check

Thesis Status Evidence
1. All-stages label is the durable moat SUPPORTED OX2 low recall / high effectiveness pattern persists; Rivfloza still restricted to eGFR ≥30; ATU shows OXLUMO chosen for ESKD hypothetical
2. Self-administration advantage will grow WATCH RI9 is the single most effective message across brands; but Q3 2025 is first competitive wave, need trend data
3. Underdiagnosis is the real competitive threat SUPPORTED 2025 genetic prevalence data (5/million vs 1-3/million); urologist familiarity gap widening; disease messages most recalled in high-impact interactions
4. Pediatric dominance holds SUPPORTED ATU: Rivfloza pediatric usage NOT expected to increase; OXLUMO dominant in pediatric treatment share; 55% nephrologists prefer OXLUMO for pediatric patient
5. Access perception gap is urgent STRENGTHENED Consistent across ALL 4 studies; non-user driven; Rivfloza access messaging actively exploiting the gap

3. Data Gaps and Research Opportunities

Gap 1: No Longitudinal Competitive Tracking Pre-Rivfloza Launch

The PET baseline (Q4 2023) preceded Rivfloza promotional activity, so there's no true pre-competitive benchmark to measure share-of-voice erosion precisely. The Q3 2025 wave is the first competitive comparison. A second competitive wave is needed to establish trend.

Gap 2: Urologist-Specific Module

Both PET and ATU include urologists but with low sample sizes (*Low sample in most analyses). Urologists are the key untapped audience for PH1 awareness. A dedicated urologist module — testing genetic testing adoption, PH1 suspicion triggers, and referral patterns — would fill a significant gap.

Gap 3: Patient/Caregiver Perspective

All current studies are HCP-facing. No patient/caregiver data on administration preference, treatment burden, quality of life, or switching drivers. Given the lifelong nature of PH1 treatment and the administration convenience debate (HCP-administered quarterly vs self-administered monthly), patient preference data would be highly valuable.

Gap 4: Real-World Switching Behavior

ATU captures treatment share at a point in time, but not dynamic switching behavior (OXLUMO → Rivfloza, Rivfloza → OXLUMO, B6 → siRNA transitions). With both products now established, a switching/persistence study would provide Alnylam with actionable retention data.

Gap 5: MSL Impact in PH1

The combined OXL+GIV PET readout shows MSL impact data for GIVLAARI (AHP), but MSL analysis for OXLUMO is limited to a few questions. Given the importance of medical education in ultra-rare disease, a dedicated MSL impact assessment for OXLUMO (similar to the Genmab Epkinly MSL study) could be a valuable add-on.

4. ZoomRx Deliverable Quality Notes

What's Working

  • Head-to-head OXLUMO vs Rivfloza message effectiveness comparison — the core competitive insight Alnylam needs
  • High-impact interaction driver analysis — identifies exactly what makes an OXLUMO detail successful
  • Disease message recall tracking (D1-D5) — connects disease awareness to promotional effectiveness
  • Hypothetical patient profiles in ATU — reveals real treatment decision-making patterns by specialty
  • Target vs off-list segmentation — demonstrates differential rep impact

What Could Improve

  • Sample sizes remain challenging across all studies (n=29-39 for OXLUMO interactions) — ultra-rare disease constraint, but confidence intervals should be more prominently flagged
  • The ATU skeletal report format has placeholder text ("xxx", "TBU") — suggesting rushed internal workflow before client delivery
  • Specialty segmentation in PET could be more pronounced — current analysis mixes nephrology and urology, potentially masking different competitive dynamics
  • No wave-over-wave trending tables in the PET final report — the baseline-to-Q3-2025 comparison should be explicitly tabulated for Alnylam's internal tracking
  • Combined OXL+GIV PET readout: the first ~200 slides are GIVLAARI/AHP content; OXLUMO content is buried. Consider separate readout decks or clear table-of-contents navigation
